Chapter 1
Biology, behaviour and husbandry
John Chitty, Bob Doneley, Anna Meredith and Anne McBride
Natural history
The domestic guinea pig (Cavia porcellus; Linnaeus, 1758), 
also referred to as the cavy, is derived from the wild Brazil-
ian cavy (Cavia aperea; Erxleben, 1777) (Figure 1.1). The 
Brazilian cavy is classified in the following manner: Order 
Rodentia, Suborder Hystricomorpha, Infraorder Hystrico-
gnathi, family Caviidae. The species is abundant through-
out northern South America, southern Brazil and northern 
Argentina and can be found in a wide range of habitats, 
principally savannah and dense grasslands. Cavies do not 
burrow but are surface dwellers. They use tunnels in the 
long grass as ‘highways’ and places to rest and nurse, and 
also make use of rock crevices and even the burrows of 
other species as retreats from predators or aggressive 
encounters with conspecifics. Their diet is primarily dif-
ferent species of grass, supplemented by seeds and flow-
ers when available.
Wild cavies tend to live in stable groups of 5–10 indi-
viduals with which they form robust lifelong social bonds 
(Asher et al., 2004; McBride and Meredith, 2019). Both 
sexes form strong linear hierarchies and are territorial, with 
substantial home ranges. The ranges of males (boars) are 
larger than and overlap those of females (sows). Depending 
on the available resources, including sows, the size of boar 
ranges varies from 165 m2 to 1387 m2. Boars may control  A Brazilian cavy in a zoological collection.
1.1

Lifespan 4–7 years
Bodyweight Sows: 700–900 g Brazilian cavies weigh 590–700 g; boars are larger than sows

Brazilian cavies have slightly smaller litters

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Example outdoor enclosure with sufficient space for 8–12 guinea pigs. Part of the roofing (over the hutches) is solid to provide 
1.4
shade and protection from rain. This is secured at the rear to prevent flapping and wind damage. Open areas are also provided to 
allow access to sunlight. The hutches have waterproof rooves and are positioned so that wind and rain do not directly blow in. Doors are 
secured open to prevent slamming and to allow constant access. The hutches are raised off the ground to reduce the risk of damp. Ramps 
and steps are provided to facilitate access. The space under the hutches also allows the guinea pigs to hide. Some hay is left outside the 
hutch so the guinea pigs can drag it under the hutch. Alternatively, tubing (e.g. drainpipes) and/or large mounds of hay to hide in could be 
provided. This group dragged any mound of hay under the hutches and did not take to drainpipes – it is important to observe animals’ 
preferences and take account of them. As guinea pigs do not climb, three-foot tall side wiring (arrowhead) is sufficient to contain them. 
While not shown in this image, it is recommended that the entire roof should be wired to avoid any risk of predation (e.g. by birds, foxes, cats 
or dogs). Alternatively, electrified fencing may be placed external to the pen. Metal construction is robust and reduces the risk of chew 
damage by the guinea pigs. The pen is secured to the ground and underwired for 30–50 cm both internally and externally. This is to prevent 
guinea pigs pushing under and escaping, and to prevent predators digging in. Heavy wooden beams assist in securing the underwiring and 
also provide additional privacy. An open area is provided to allow limited grazing as well as space for exercise and sunbathing. Enrichment 
and hides may be placed in this area. However, provided there are sufficient and easily accessed hiding places elsewhere, guinea pigs will 
spend a lot of time in the open. Scatter feeding also assists in providing enrichment, and space should be allowed for feeding enrichment 
devices. There should also be plenty of water bowls, both in the open areas and the hutches. Bowls of pelleted food should be provided 
under cover to prevent rain spoilage. Food and water bowls should be solid and difficult to tip over. Ideally, they should also be frost 
resistant and some should be positioned out of direct sunlight and in areas more protected from freezing.
(© John Chitty)

1.6
enclosure will provide additional shade in the summer 
and further security for the guinea pigs by screening them from 
overhead predators.
(© John Chitty)

Chapter 1 • Biology, behaviour and husbandry
Normal behaviour
The normal behaviour of the guinea pig has evolved to 
meet their needs for survival in a particular ecosystem. 
Guinea pigs use body movements and vocalizations to 
express their emotional state and intention (McBride and 
Hinde-Megarity, 2022).
Vocalizations
Guinea pigs have an extensive repertoire of vocalizations, 
which they use to communicate with one another (Berry-
man, 1976). These include loud ‘wheeks’ or whistle calls 
for location (‘Here I am, where are you?’) and demonstrat-
ing excitement, or rapid chirping to alert others to danger. 
Outdoor scatter feeding provides enrichment. Problems 
There are also quiet sounds made between a sow and her  1.7

understanding the vocalizations of guinea pigs is available  may reduce the effects of poaching in the short term.
from Oxbow Animal Health (see ‘Useful websites’, below). (© John Chitty)

Breeding
Social groups
Most guinea pigs are kept as companion animals, with 
the owner having no intention to breed the animals. 
Guinea pigs can be kept very successfully in same-sex 
groups and this avoids the need for neutering. However, 
if the animals are to be kept in a mixed-sex pair or group, 
veterinary advice should be sought about the options 
for neutering (see ‘Surgical neutering of guinea pigs – 
ethical aspects’, below, and Chapters 12 and 18). Inad-
vertent co-housing of mis-sexed individuals or 
postpubertal boars and sows will inevitably occur on 
occasion. Breeders and sellers should know how to sex 
Ventral aspect of a sow. prepubertal guinea pigs. Ideally, breeding should be

between 6 and 9 months of age. Sows that have their 
first litter after the pubic symphysis has fused may be at 
increased risk of dystocia; however, this is not inevitable 
and, in the authors’ collective experience, is a rare occur-
rence. Sows reach puberty, and can be bred from, at 
approximately 500 g bodyweight (5–8 weeks of age) 
(Harkness and Wagner, 2010). Boars will start demon-
strating mounting behaviour as early as 4 weeks of age, 
with intromissions occurring at around 6 weeks; however, 
they are usually not fertile until 8–10 weeks of age 
(Gresham and Haines, 2012). Boars are first used for 
breeding when their bodyweight reaches 500–800 g 
(usually at 7–13 weeks) (Shomer et al., 2015).
